articles.title: Loco Pilots Hold Nationwide Protest Over Non-AC Locomotives, Extreme Heat Conditions - ETV Bharat
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
Representational image (ANI)
New Delhi: Loco Pilots (LPs) and Assistant Loco Pilots (ALPs) on Friday staged a nationwide protest titled "Mundi Garam" against the lack of air-conditioning and lavatory facilities in a majority of Indian Railways locomotives. The protestors alleged that train drivers are being forced to work in inhuman conditions during extreme summer temperatures.
The protesters raised the issue of working in harsh conditions inside locomotive cabins, where temperatures reportedly rise beyond 50 degrees Celsius during peak summer months. Railway staff said that nearly 60 per cent of locomotives in the Indian Railways network are still non-air-conditioned.
According to protesting LPs and ALPs, Indian Railways currently operates over 15,000 locomotives, many of which force drivers to work inside confined metal cabins exposed to direct heat for long hours.
While some locomotives are equipped with air-conditioning systems, railway staff alleged that several AC units are either poorly maintained or non-functional.
Ram Raj Bhagat, President of the All India Loco Running Staff Association (AILRSA), said loco pilots are regularly compelled to work in cabins that become heat-trapped metal enclosures under high temperatures.
"Such conditions are not just uncomfortable but can directly affect the health, endurance and alertness required for safe train operations," Bhagat told ETV Bharat. He added that the grievances of loco pilots and assistant loco pilots had already been raised before the Railway Board and senior railway authorities, but no concrete action had been taken so far.
The association said prolonged exposure to extreme heat inside locomotive cabins is causing serious physical and mental stress among train crew members. Ram Sharam, Central President of AILRSA, said train crew members were left with no option but to protest against non-air-conditioned locomotives.
"The protest is due to the growing frustration among railway staff who feel that prolonged exposure to high temperatures inside metal cabins is affecting their well-being, while also increasing the strain of maintaining constant alertness during train operations," he said.
Railway staff further alleged that non-standard cabin fans installed in locomotives create excessive noise and do not provide proper airflow. They also complained about uncomfortable seating arrangements inside cabins.
A loco pilot posted in Northern Railway, speaking on condition of anonymity, said loco running staff submitted memorandums to Divisional Railway Managers and General Managers demanding immediate installation of air-conditioning systems and lavatories in locomotives.
"Heatwaves have already started, but AC facilities have not been properly provided in locos. In many locomotives where AC exists, the systems are not functioning. It becomes extremely difficult to work when cabin temperatures exceed 50 degrees Celsius," the loco pilot said.
The memorandum also stressed the need for lavatory facilities in locomotives to maintain punctuality, dignity and health of train drivers, particularly as Indian Railways is now employing women loco pilots.
Loco pilots said that their work requires constant alertness, precision and mental endurance as they are responsible for the safe movement of thousands of passengers daily. They said that inadequate cooling systems and prolonged exposure to extreme heat could eventually affect operational efficiency and safety.
